topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating monotony, mirroring the dull ache behind Alex’s eyes. He stared blankly at the spreadsheet, the numbers blurring into meaningless streaks of black and white. Then, the email landed. A salary comparison, stark and undeniable. A slow, cold knot tightened in his stomach. He traced the text with a fingertip, the movement heavy and deliberate. He felt a strange pressure building behind his ribs, a tightness he hadn't recognized before. When Sarah, the junior analyst, finally approached his desk, offering a tentative smile, Alex just managed a tight nod. "Oh, hey, Sarah. Yeah, I saw that." His voice came out flat, devoid of any warmth. He picked up his lukewarm coffee, the ceramic feeling slick in his hand, and took a long, slow sip, trying to quell the sudden, unwelcome churning inside him.

[story 2]

Mark leaned back in his ergonomic chair, the leather squeaking in protest under his weight. He kept rubbing the bridge of his nose, a repetitive, unconscious gesture. The data report had been undeniable, a fact presented in crisp, unfeeling figures. A heavy lethargy settled over him, making every movement feel like wading through thick mud. He scrolled through the pay structure again, the discrepancy between his own take-home and that of young Kevin was glaring. A low sigh escaped him, a sound more of deflation than breath. When Kevin walked past his cubicle, whistling tunelessly, Mark kept his gaze fixed on his monitor, refusing to meet the young man’s eyes. He just offered a barely perceptible shrug, a gesture that communicated volumes of internal dissatisfaction without uttering a single word.

[story 3]

Eliza picked at a loose thread on her sweater, her gaze fixed on the beige carpet as if it held the secrets of the universe. The realization hit her not with a bang, but with a slow, creeping realization, like a cold draft seeping under a closed door. Her jaw tightened almost involuntarily. The numbers on the screen confirmed what she had suspected, a quiet, simmering resentment rising in her chest. She didn't look up when Ben, a newer team member, approached her to ask about the quarterly projections. "Uh, they're fine," she murmured, her tone clipped and distant. Her hands clenched into fists beneath the desk, the tension radiating outward in a subtle rigidity. She focused intently on a dust mote dancing in a shaft of sunlight, anything to avoid acknowledging the unsettling truth.
